coredata.dc:title: Visible-light-induced aerobic C-3 thiocyanation of indoles using carbon dots as photoredox catalyst

Bugde, Sandesh
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we demonstrate the use of rose-bengal derived carbon dots for C-3 thiocyanation of indoles. These carbon dots serve as an non-toxic, biocompatible and environmentally friendly catalysts for the C-3 thiocyanation reaction. To our best knowledge, this is the first report to use rose-bengal derived carbon dots for C-3 thiocyanation of indoles. The yield of the synthesized derivatives of indoles were found to be good to excellent in the range of 33 % to 84 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible-light
Photocatalysts
Carbon dots
Thiocyanation
Indole

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Examples of biologically active organosulfur compounds.
HR-TEM images of RB-CDs a) 50 nm, b) 10 nm c) 5 nm; d) The size distribution histogram of RB-CDs; e) FTIR spectroscopy of RB-CDs.
a) UV–Vis absorption and b) PL emission spectra of RB-CDs.
C-3 thiocyanation of indoles using carbon dots (RB-CDs) as photoredox catalyst
Synthesis of carbon dots using rose Bengal and o-phenylenediamine
C-3 thiocyanation of indoles using carbon dots (CD) as photoredox catalyst
Plausible mechanism for C-3 thiocyanation of indoles using carbon dots (CD) as photoredox catalyst

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: Indole 1 (0.85 mmol),ammonium thiocyanate (3 eq.), CD (20 wt%), THF (4 mL) at room temperature irradiated under 1 W blue LEDs for 24 h.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: Indole 1 (0.85 mmol), ammonium thiocyanate (3 eq.), CD (20 wt%), Solvent (4 mL) at room temperature irradiated under 1 W blue LEDs for 24 h aKSCN.

head.ce:title: Visible-light-induced aerobic C-3 thiocyanation of indoles using carbon dots as photoredox catalyst
head.ce:dedication: This paper is dedicated to Professor Santosh G. Tilve in celebration of his 66th birthday on 2nd March 2025 in recognition of his outstanding contributions to the field of Organic Chemistry, as well as his dedication to teaching and research.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Goa University, Taleigao Plateau, Goa 403 206, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Goa University, Taleigao Plateau, Goa 403 206, INDIA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we demonstrate the use of rose-bengal derived carbon dots for C-3 thiocyanation of indoles. These carbon dots serve as an non-toxic, biocompatible and environmentally friendly catalysts for the C-3 thiocyanation reaction. To our best knowledge, this is the first report to use rose-bengal derived carbon dots for C-3 thiocyanation of indoles. The yield of the synthesized derivatives of indoles were found to be good to excellent in the range of 33 % to 84 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data supporting the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organosulfur compounds are an essential class due to their diverse biological and pharmaceutical properties, making them valuable scaffolds in medicinal chemistry. Some representative examples include arylthioindole, albendazole, and captodiamine derivatives, as shown in Fig. 1 . Consequently, significant efforts have been made to incorporate sulfur groups into organic frameworks. Among various methods, direct thiocyanation of aromatic and heteroaromatic compounds offers a convenient approach for carbon−sulfur bond formation. Thiocyanation products, known as thiocyanates, serve as valuable intermediates in synthesizing sulfur-containing heterocycles. Additionally, thiocyanates are versatile precursors for synthesizing various organosulfur compounds, such as thiols, thioethers, thioesters, trifluoromethylthiolates, thiocarbamates, isothiocyanates, disulfides, sulfur-based heterocycles, and pharmaceuticals [1–6].

In literature various methods have been used for thiocyanation reaction, utilizing different acid-base catalysts and reagents like graphene oxide, Al₂O₃/MeSO₃H, trichloroisocyanuric acid, p-TSA, montmorillonite K-10 clay, N-thiocyanosuccinimide, citric acid, antimony (V) chloride, lead (II) thiocyanate, and aryl thallium bistrifluoroacetate. Other approaches involve Lewis acid such as Cu(OTf)2, AlCl3 etc., and photocatalysts such as eosin Y, rose bengal, merrifield resin-rose bengal, g-C3N4, Ag/TiO2, fluorescein, COF, merrifield resin-RB, Ir(II) etc. Metal based catalysts like TiO2/MoS2, CuCl2.2H2O, as well as oxidizing agents such as CAN, DDQ, oxone, H2O2, H5IO6, K2S2O8, Poly(4-diacetoxyiodo)styrene, Cu/O2, Mn(OAc)3, IBX, iodic acid, have also been applied, along with electrolysis and mechanochemical methods (see Fig. 1) [3,7–19]. Despite their efficiencies, these oxidants/reagents suffered from many drawbacks and limitations such as stoichiometric oxidants, generation of metal waste, strong acidic conditions, generation of toxic byproducts and high catalyst costs. Consequently, there is a need to develop a more sustainable, metal-free catalyst for thiocyanation reactions.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of fluorescent nano carbon-based nanomaterials having size less than 10 nm [20–24]. They have received a lot of attention due to their unique fascinating features and vast range of applications in the field of sensors, drug delivery, phototherapy, bioimaging, energy storage systems, optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, and antimicrobial protection. CDs have characteristics such as minimal toxicity, environmental friendly, high photostability, excellent luminescence, and biocompatibility. Until now, CDs were synthesized from a variety of precursors, but researchers are looking for new precursors to boost the luminous property, which is reduced by passivating agents and hazardous compounds [25–31].

Recently, quantum dots have been employed as substitutes for dyes and metal-based photoredox catalysts. A few studies report the use of ZnSe/CdS core/shell quantum dots in organic reactions, such as CC coupling, nitrobenzene reduction, and reductive dehalogenation [32,33]. Quantum dots, especially carbon quantum dots, are being explored as photocatalysts; however, their effectiveness in organic reactions involving photoinduced electron transfer (PET) behavior is not yet fully understood [34,35]. Most carbon dots are soluble in both water and organic solvents, making them ideal as homogeneous catalysts for organic reactions. Carbon dots have been used for oxidation reaction, CN coupling of amines to imines and 3,4-dihydroisoquinoline synthesis [36–39]. Additionally, DDQ-derived carbon dots (CDs) have been used in Csp2–Csp2, Csp2–B, Csp2–Se, Csp2–S, and CP bond-forming reactions. Utilizing functionalized quantum dots could enhance photoredox properties, which would be beneficial for organic reactions [40].

Visible-light-driven photocatalytic transformations have shown promising results in producing 3-substituted indoles. The use of visible light for direct C-3 functionalization of indoles offers significant advantages over transition metal catalysts, particularly regarding environmental impact and cost efficiency. Driven by our interest in photoredox strategies and the carbon dots as photocatalysts, we chose to use the literature method to synthesize carbon dots derived from rose bengal and o-phenylenediamine, through a bottom-up hydrothermal approach for the C-3 thiocyanation of indoles.(Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rose-bengal derived carbon dots (RB-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: We followed the carbon dots synthesis method as Li et al. [41] reported. To prepare RB-CDs, rose bengal (0.13 g) and 1,2-phenylenediamine (0.13 g) were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) and placed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and was heated to 180 °C for 10 h. After that, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and solvent was removed under vacuum. The obtained solid was then dissolved in ethyl acetate (20 mL) and extracted twice with water (20 mL × 2). The organic layer was combined and dried using anhydrous sodium sulphate. The organic layer was evaporated under vaccum using rota-vap, yielding the reddish-brown solid CDs. The crude CDs was further purified by silica gel chromatography (60–120 mesh).(Scheme 2 ).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) studies of RB-CDs reveal the formation of quasi-spherical, monodisperse particles with a size distribution ranging from 0.8 to 2.8 nm (Fig. 2 a-d). FTIR spectroscopy were carried out to identify the surface functionalities of RB-CDs. The peak at 3336 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups. The peaks at 2973 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of CH bonds. The peak at 1614 cm−1 is associated with CC stretching. Additionally, the absorption band between 1268 cm−1 and 1172 cm−1 corresponds to CN and CO stretching vibrations (Fig. 2e). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of RB-CDs displayed a peak at 435 nm, attributed to the π–π* transition of the large aromatic sp2 core within the RB-CDs. The RB-CDs solution exhibited red-shifted emission when excited at wavelengths between 412 nm and 492 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased, the maximum emission progressively shifted to longer wavelengths. This variation in fluorescence behavior at different excitation wavelengths may be attributed to the presence of distinct particle sizes and surface energy traps within the CDs. Additionally, the fluorescence quantum yield was found to be 44.3 %. (See Fig. 3.)

The purified carbon dots (RB-CDs) used in the thiocyanation reaction. To our knowledge, using carbon dots as photocatalysts for the C-3 functionalization of indoles has not yet been reported, so we aimed to explore their potential in the C-3 thiocyanation of indoles.

The control experiments were carried out to confirm that the reaction proceeds via a photoredox pathway (Table 1). These tests showed that compound 2a does not form without light (Table 1, entry 2). When the reaction was performed with light but without catalyst, the yield was only 26 %. Additionally, when the reaction was carried out under a nitrogen atmosphere, the yield decreased to 17 %, underscoring the essential role of oxygen in facilitating the reaction. These results demonstrate that the catalyst and light are essential for this reaction to follow a photochemical process.

To understand the impact of different solvents, we tested solvents like acetonitrile (MeCN), methanol (MeOH), dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), chloroform (CHCl₃), and tetrahydrofuran (THF) with 20 wt% carbon dots (Table 2 , entries 1–10). This screening found that THF was the most effective solvent for the C-3 thiocyanation of indole (Table 2, entry 4). We also tried using KSCN as the thiocyanating agent instead of NH₄SCN, which resulted in a significantly lower yield (Table 2, entry 5).

Under optimized conditions, we expanded the reaction's scope to synthesize various derivatives using different functional groups. Indoles with electron-donating groups, like 5-methoxy (5-OMe), produced moderate yields of 3-thiocyanoindoles (52 %, Scheme 4 ), 2g. The reaction also worked well with indoles containing weak electron-withdrawing groups (Scheme 4, 2d–2f). However, no product was formed with indoles containing strongly electron-withdrawing nitro groups (Scheme 4, 2j). Similarly, tert-butyl 1H-indole-1-carboxylate did not react, likely due to the electron-withdrawing nature of the Boc group (Scheme 4, 2i). When an indole with a phenyl group at the C-2 position was used, product 2c formed with a 59 % yield, showing that C-2 steric hindrance did not impact the reaction. This was also observed with an indole having a methyl group at the C-2 position, which yielded 72 % of product 2b (Scheme 4). A comparative study of the current method was conducted with existing conventional thiocyanation protocols and other similar approaches (Table S1, See Supporting Information).

In order to understand the mechanistic aspects few experiments were performed (Scheme 5 ). As anticipated, the reaction of indole and thiocyanate with three equivalents of 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidyl-1-oxyl (TEMPO), a radical scavenger, resulted in a low 5 % yield of the desired product 2sp (Scheme 5, 1S). Under optimized conditions, 3-methylindole (Scheme 5, 2P) failed to give any product, this suggests the reaction occurs at C-3 active site only.

Based on our observations and literature reports [42], a plausible mechanism for this process is illustrated in Scheme 6 . When exposed to visible light, carbon dots (CD, 3a) enters a long-lived excited state (CD*, 3b). A single electron transfer occurs between the thiocyanate ion (−SCN, 4a) and CD* (3b), producing the ·SCN radical (4b) and the CD radical anion (CD・-, 3c). The photoredox cycle is completed when molecular oxygen oxidizes the CD radical anion (3c) back to the ground state CD (3a). Following this, the ·SCN radical (4b) undergoes electrophilic addition to compound 1a, resulting in intermediate 5. Intermediate 5 is then oxidized to form a positively charged intermediate (6). Finally, intermediate 6 loses a proton and re-aromatizes, yielding the final product, 3-thiocyanoindole (2a).

To a solution of indole 1 (0.13 mmol) and ammonium thiocyanate (3 equivalents) in THF (4 mL), carbon dots (20 wt%) were added. The reaction mixture was exposed to air and stirred at room temperature under a 1 W (SynLED) blue LED light. Upon completion of the reaction, the mixture was extracted with ethyl acetate (20 mL × 3) and water. The combined organic extracts were dried over anhydrous sodium sulfate (Na₂SO₄), and the solvent was removed using a rotary evaporator. The product was purified using silica gel column chromatography (60–120 mesh) with a petroleum ether/ethyl acetate mixture (8:2) to yield the desired 3-thiocyanoindole product. The reactions were performed in triplicates to verify reproducibility for the formation of 3-thiocyanoindole.

In summary, we have developed a simple and efficient thiocyanation strategy that facilitates CS bond formation using carbon dots as a photoredox catalyst. This approach provides a convenient route to synthesize 3-thiocyanato-1H-indole derivatives. The environmentally friendly nature of carbon dots provides an advantage over other synthesis methods. Various electron-rich arenes demonstrated high effectiveness for this transformation, with substrate yields ranging from 33 % to 84 %.

Pritesh Khobrekar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sandesh Bugde: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Goa University providing different spectroscopic techniques. We Acknowledge DST-FIST, DST-PURSE and UGC-SAP for instrumentation facility at SCS, Goa University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material. C-3 thiocyanation of indoles using rose bengal carbon dots..
